PRESIDENT ROOSEVELTS CABINET
.>4 THE SECRETARY OF THE. INTERIOR

ny y. ellison Gardner.

pages.raw_text:
Etan Correspondence of the News


paper Enterprise Association. 2.


WASHINGTON, March 2S.-The
enemies of Ethan Allen Hitchcock
will have to put up with his presence
in the cabinet for year longer at
least. There are still some land frauds
TO be uncovered, and there are several
senate and house of representatives
still under suspicion.


The honorable secretary of the in
terror is a singed cat. He is not SO
bad as his exterior would indicate
He is cross and crotchety and as
fussy as a steam roller. lie is about
as poor an executive as could be
found among all the government en-
ployes; he is suspicious. pompous, in
tolerant and altogether disagreeable


And yet he is the most valuable
member of the Roosevelt administra-
ton. He may be forgiven all, for he
is honest. AS a government official
he is against graft, and during the
three years of his administration of
the interior department he has set
his face like flint against iniquity in
high places.


But it is a glorious pleasure to hear
him consign the United States sena-
tors to hell, and an equally large JOY
to hear the United States senators
pay their loving tributes to him.


DO you think Hitchcock is a -
] started to ask.


He is all that and more" came
back before l could specify. And
then there were SMILES and refer
/ences which were almost pious in
their blasphemous earnestness. II
you want to witness something lurid
take one of those northwest senators
into an embrasure of the marble room
and ask him what he thinks of the
secretary of the interior.


The interior department is-or has
been-the breeding place for as much
graft as all the rest of the depart
ments put together. 1t is a vast anti
quatcd machine, which has to do witt
the government lands, the forests
and the big irrigation projects which
have been inaugurated during the
past three years.


A year of Hitchcock convinced tn.
powers of congress and some other
political powers that he was not &
suitable man for the place. If there
had been a less honest or less obsti
nate man in the White House, it is
safe to conclude that they would have
accomplished his exit. But Roose.
